item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
What this puts me in mind of (besides weird math things like SCP-1314, SCP-3141 and everything else with the mathematical tag) is SCP-1958.

Notably the propulsion system for the van is unexplained and both articles have similar themes of naive applications of weird science causing deaths in space. Also the idea that unconventional math results in aphysical events seems to be thematically close to the Fifth Church's experience of altered reality by rejection of conventional reason.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I agree it was pretty loose. Reverted.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: As the author of the work being cross-linked to, I don't really see the connection that either of you are making, nor do I approve of this particular crosslink. As much as I love crosslinks, I'd rather not have my stuff shoehorned into unrelated skips.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I think your suggestion works better than mine. I don't think SCP-225 would work as a link, though.

Though I am confused about what you're asking for. Do you want me to replace the text in the table cell with this text?

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
I can see the Prometheus FTL link, but putting the link right the might be confusing since the spacecraft is the one in the log.

Maybe

A capsule and four crew intended to test inertia-free launch conditions for manned spaceflight.

Also if you or anyone can think of SCPs the Foundation might have tried this out on by accident, that would be an easy work-in. I thought about SCP-225, for example, but back then it felt like coattail-hanging.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: edits made at suggestion of the author

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This is a pretty small, self-contained story. However, I think there's a room for a crosslink here. There's an allusion to spaceships here, and I think I can fit in a Prometheus Labs tale about it.

Editing this:

A manned spacecraft intended to test inertia-free launch conditions, with four crew.

To this:

A manned spacecraft intended to test inertia-free launch conditions, with four crew.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this. The final log was weird, and I didn't quite understand what was going on — but it still sold it for me. The idea of an after-image being left behind (and having to be contained by the Foundation) is very cool. I also like a lot of the subtle jokes/nods to physics vs engineering, here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alpha, Beta, Gamma, and [DATA EXPUNGED] particles.

I only have a very basic grasp of this kind of stuff, but wouldn't they be gamma rays? Unless there's a type of high-level-science gamma particle that I'm not aware of :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it. +1

One comment: MTF Psi-8 is an established one, the Silencers. They're on the Task Forces abridged list and everything. I like the idea of the Tenure Trackers, though- I would keep them, just change the designation.

If you know what I mean ( ͡° ͜ʖ ͡°)

Damnit this font tho

It messes up my lenny face ;-;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Took me a second read to (somewhat) understand this, but even after the first one it was pretty neat. As somebody said upthread, these things that are not some creature of object but just something like a "glitch" in the universe are always interesting.
The last log was also neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is quite excellent. I'm guessing that one of the side-effects of the Type-D drive was immortality. Or rather, I guess, time doesn't pass for them anymore in the way we understand. Loved the logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't care for the log, but the description sold me so hard that I upvoted regardless.
Basically, "Boss, I think we broke mathematics." XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Graphical representation solution fascinates me. Also the addendum of the types of incidents is brilliant. Really complex effect and idea to explain, but you did!
I like this SCP, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed and changed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't like the log much, but the effect is novel. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"I don't think we'll be back" indeed. If I had one complaint it's that you telegraph that line with the one before it ("Goodbye, my friends, this will be my last log."). At first this felt a little boring, but as a sciency SCP I was still interested, then the final addendum blew me away.

Edit: You'd get more emotional punch out of "Goodbye, my friends, I don't think we'll be back."

Well done. +1.

item.title: Yaaaaaaaay
item.content:encoded: Yaaaaaaaay

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no idea what's going on, but I somehow still enjoyed reading it. I find myself often gravitating towards SCP entries that aren't about some beast or phenomena that's out to get us in the end, and I also like the idea that there are some poor Foundation researchers whose lot in life is to deal with math that doesn't play well with others.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It does say (Unconfirmed) :P

Admittedly, I didn't know that bit, although I had heard people talking about uniform fourth-dimensional expansion. If anyone has a good empirical suggestion I could change that to, I wouldn't object.

Edit: Changed it after a slight bit of googling. Debating embedding a link: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lema%C3%AEtre%E2%80%93Tolman_metric

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, so, for the part in the description, that was supposed to make sense.

The objects are stuck in space compared to something other than Earth. Type A's are stuck compared to the Sun, so they're off in space most of the time. However, they're still in the path of Earth's orbit, so Earth passes back through them once a year. B's and C's are stuck compared to the galaxy and universe, so because the solar system is constantly moving compared to those, from our perspective they're just flying off into space at absurd speeds.

If there's a way to get that across more clearly, let me know and I can try to reword it. I struggled with trying to describe that.

As for the capsule crew...
- hide this content

What's happening to them is based on a weird daydream/misunderstanding I had in a course on special relativity. This is 100% not how the actual physics work, but here's how the thought goes:
Compared to the Earth, they are moving away at very, very close to the speed of light. What they're actually seeing around them is just a corona of light reflected from one side of the Earth. They're constantly almost catching up with Earth's light, but not quite, which is why they see things changing slowly instead of just staying the same.

(Or, viewed the other way, the light from Earth is constantly passing by them, but very slowly).

That's why they can't see one hemisphere or anything that was blocked by clouds: There's no light reflecting from those areas in their direction.

As for the rest of it, I figure they can fly around and ignore the laws of physics because of the device's effects. As for the planets, well, it was a daydream :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would upvote except from my understanding there is no "center" of the universe

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't really understand it, but then again I don't really understand math at all, so I can't tell if it's vague or I'm just dumb.

What does "objects are fixed in place in comparison with some other astronomical object or reference point, and remain in this position with no regard for the movement of the Earth" mean? Are the objects in space or on Earth? Do the subjects of the log exist on Earth as sort of physics ghosts for whom the perception of time is accelerated? What does it mean when they say that a hemisphere has disappeared behind them? Why can they leave to explore space? The worst part is that I have no idea which if any of these are even meant to be understood, if them being half-incomprehensible is the point, or I'm just missing things, or they need to be explained differently.

Regardless, I like it. I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, here goes nothing! Image is mine, heavily edited from public domain images.

Thanks to Michael Atreus and especially Kalinin for feedback, as well as a few other folks on the forum.

If anyone has any clarity or grammar issues, I'll try to address them quickly.

Edit: Also, thanks to Michael Atreus for the use of the ECRG. I figure anyone capable of taking a "wrong" answer as far as these probably belongs there. :)
